methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $15M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Housing & Construction (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 1 same-sector peers exist in Housing & Construction, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Inversion Space
private.rationale: Same sector (Housing & Construction), same stage (Series A)

private.name: Cobod
private.rationale: Same sector (Housing & Construction), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Material Evolution, founded by Liz Gilligan and Sam Clark, is a Series A-stage company in Housing & Construction that has raised $15M to date. The relevant peer universe consists of three private companies (ICON, Inversion Space, and Cobod) operating in the same sector; no public comparables exist in this space, and the narrow peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage datapoints to support a robust peer-median valuation method.

The Stage-Progression method is the primary applicable triangulation approach. Using total capital raised ($15M) as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x multiplier typical of Series A progression to subsequent funding rounds yields an implied valuation of $30M. This reflects standard step-up dynamics for companies at this stage.

The Peer-Median method is not applicable due to insufficient comparable data; filtering for same-stage or similar-tier peers in Housing & Construction returned fewer than three observations, making a median-based approach unreliable and potentially misleading. Accordingly, the analysis relies on a single method. The triangulated range supports a valuation of $26M (low) to $34M (high), with a median point of $30M. Key uncertainties include the absence of public market reference points, limited availability of narrow-stage private peers, and Material Evolution's specific unit economics relative to the identified peer set.
